class_schedule.topic: Introduction to HCI and Usability Engineering
learning_objectives: Understand the goals and methods of human-computer interaction
Apply Nielsen's usability heuristics to evaluate existing interfaces
Identify common usability problems in real-world software
readings: Norman, The Design of Everyday Things, Ch. 1-2
Nielsen, '10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design'

lecture_notes.introduction: Great software that nobody can use is failed software. HCI bridges psychology, design, and engineering to create interfaces that are effective, efficient, and satisfying. This course teaches you to design with the user, not just for the user.
key_concepts: Usability: learnability, efficiency, memorability, errors, satisfaction

Nielsen's 10 heuristics: visibility of system status, match with real world, user control, consistency, error prevention, recognition over recall, flexibility, aesthetic design, error recovery, help and documentation
Mental models: how users think the system works vs how it actually works
Affordances and signifiers: what actions are possible and how users discover them
Human cognitive limitations: attention, working memory, decision fatigue
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a heuristic evaluation of a popular mobile app, identifying at least 5 usability violations with severity ratings and improvement suggestions

review.value: The gap between what the user wants to do and what the interface allows them to do; good design minimizes this by making actions visible, intuitive, and forgiving of mistakes
Users should not have to remember information from one screen to use another; options, actions, and context should be visible or easily retrievable rather than requiring recall from memory

learning_objectives: Conduct user interviews and contextual inquiries
Synthesize research findings into personas and user journey maps
Translate user needs into design requirements
readings: Kuniavsky, Observing the User Experience, Ch. 5-7

lecture_notes.introduction: Designing for real users requires understanding real users. User research replaces assumptions with evidence. Personas and journey maps are tools for keeping the team focused on user needs throughout development.
key_concepts: User interview techniques: open-ended questions, active listening, follow-up probing
Contextual inquiry: observing users in their natural environment
Affinity diagramming: organizing research findings into themes
Personas: composite user profiles based on research patterns
User journey maps: visualizing the end-to-end user experience with pain points
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a mock user interview about a task management workflow, synthesize findings with affinity diagramming, and create a persona with a journey map

review.value: A persona based on 5-8 user interviews captures the most common patterns; personas based on assumptions or demographics alone are fictional characters that do not predict real behavior

Instead of asking what features users want, ask what job they are trying to accomplish; the same job can be served by radically different solutions, and understanding the job reveals opportunities that feature requests miss

learning_objectives: Design clear navigation structures and content hierarchies
Apply interaction design patterns for common tasks
Create wireframes that communicate structure without visual distraction

lecture_notes.introduction: Information architecture determines how content is organized and labeled. Interaction design determines how users complete tasks. Together, they define the skeleton of your interface before any visual design begins.
key_concepts: Information architecture: organization, labeling, navigation, search
Card sorting: user-driven categorization for navigation design
Interaction design patterns: navigation, forms, search, filtering, wizard flows
Wireframing: low-fidelity structure-focused prototypes
Fitts's Law: larger and closer targets are faster to click; implications for button placement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design the information architecture for a recipe website using card sorting results, create a sitemap, then wireframe the key screens showing navigation and interaction patterns
setup_requirements: Card sorting tool (OptimalSort or physical cards)

review.value: The time to reach a target is proportional to the distance divided by the target size; this means important actions should be large and positioned where users already have their cursor or finger
Show only the information and controls relevant to the current task; advanced options are hidden until requested. This reduces cognitive load for common tasks while still supporting power users

learning_objectives: Plan and conduct moderated usability tests
Analyze usability test results and prioritize findings
readings: Krug, Rocket Surgery Made Easy, Ch. 1-6

lecture_notes.introduction: You cannot evaluate a design by looking at it; you must watch real users try to use it. Usability testing is the most reliable method for finding usability problems, and prototyping makes testing possible before writing code.
key_concepts: Prototype fidelity spectrum: paper sketches to interactive mockups
Figma prototyping: linking screens, adding interactions, transitions
Usability test planning: tasks, participants, metrics, think-aloud protocol
Moderated vs unmoderated testing: trade-offs in insight vs scale
Analyzing results: task success rate, time on task, error rate, SUS score
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a clickable Figma prototype of a checkout flow, then conduct a live usability test with a volunteer participant using the think-aloud protocol
setup_requirements: Usability test script and task list

review.value: Research shows that 5 users find approximately 80% of usability problems; testing with 5 users in three rounds (test, fix, retest) is more effective than testing with 15 users once

Ask participants to verbalize their thoughts as they complete tasks; this reveals what users expect, where they get confused, and what mental models they apply, providing the 'why' behind observed behavior

learning_objectives: Apply WCAG 2.1 guidelines to make web interfaces accessible
Test interfaces with screen readers and keyboard navigation
Design for diverse abilities, devices, and contexts
readings: W3C Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1 Overview

lecture_notes.introduction: Accessibility is not a feature; it is a quality attribute of all software. Over 1 billion people worldwide have disabilities. Designing for accessibility improves the experience for everyone: curb cuts benefit wheelchair users, parents with strollers, and delivery workers with carts.
key_concepts: WCAG principles: Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust
Color contrast: minimum 4.5:1 ratio for normal text
Keyboard navigation: all functionality accessible without a mouse
Screen reader compatibility: semantic HTML, ARIA labels, alt text
Inclusive design: designing for the full range of human diversity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Audit a web application for accessibility violations using axe DevTools and VoiceOver screen reader, fix the top 5 issues, and verify with retesting

review.value: Designing for people with disabilities often benefits everyone: captions help in noisy environments, high contrast helps in sunlight, keyboard shortcuts help power users, and simple language helps non-native speakers

Semantic HTML elements (button, nav, main, form, label) provide built-in accessibility; ARIA attributes should only be used when native HTML elements cannot achieve the required semantics. Incorrect ARIA is worse than no ARIA

questions.question: A user clicks a 'Save' button and nothing visible happens for 3 seconds while the server processes the request. Identify which Nielsen heuristic is violated and describe two ways to fix it.
questions.answer: Violated heuristic: Visibility of system status. The system should always keep users informed about what is happening through appropriate feedback within reasonable time. Fixes: (1) Show a loading spinner or progress indicator immediately on click, so the user knows the save is in progress. (2) Disable the Save button and change its text to 'Saving...' to prevent duplicate submissions and communicate the current state. Additionally, show a success confirmation (e.g., a brief toast notification) when the save completes.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies visibility of system status

questions.question: You are designing a new feature. A stakeholder says 'I know what users want, we don't need research.' Give three specific arguments for why user research is necessary despite stakeholder confidence.
questions.answer: 1. Stakeholders are not users: they have deep domain knowledge that actual users lack, creating a curse of knowledge that blinds them to usability issues. What seems obvious to an expert is confusing to a novice. 2. Assumptions vs evidence: stakeholder opinions are hypotheses, not facts. User research tests these hypotheses with real behavior data. Many confident assumptions are proven wrong by watching 5 users. 3. Edge cases and contexts: stakeholders think about primary use cases; user research reveals diverse contexts (slow networks, small screens, assistive technology, non-native language) that affect design decisions.

rubric.key: Arguments are grounded in HCI principles

questions.question: Design a usability test plan for a flight booking website. Include: target participants, 3 task scenarios, metrics to collect, and the think-aloud instructions you would give participants.
questions.answer: Participants: 5 adults who have booked flights online in the past year, mix of frequent and occasional travelers. Tasks: (1) Find and book the cheapest round-trip flight from Chicago to Miami for specific dates. (2) Add a checked bag to an existing booking. (3) Change the departure date of a booked flight. Metrics: Task completion rate, time on task, number of errors, post-task difficulty rating (1-5), System Usability Scale (SUS) survey. Think-aloud instructions: 'As you work through each task, please think out loud. Tell me what you are looking at, what you are thinking, and what you expect to happen when you click something. There are no wrong answers and we are testing the website, not you.'

questions.question: A web form has red error messages that appear next to fields with invalid input. Explain why this design may fail the WCAG accessibility guidelines and how to fix it.
questions.answer: Problems: (1) Color alone: using only red color to indicate errors fails WCAG 1.4.1 (Use of Color); color-blind users may not perceive the red. (2) Screen reader access: if error messages are injected dynamically without ARIA live regions, screen readers will not announce them. Fixes: (1) Add an error icon and descriptive text alongside the color change (do not rely on color alone). (2) Use aria-describedby to associate error messages with their form fields, and aria-live='polite' on the error container so screen readers announce new errors. (3) Ensure error text meets 4.5:1 contrast ratio against its background.

rubric.key: Identifies color-only indication as the accessibility issue

major_assignments.title: User-Centered Design Project
major_assignments.description: Conduct a complete user-centered design process for a real application: user research, persona creation, information architecture, wireframing, interactive prototyping, usability testing, and iterative refinement based on test results.
objectives: Conduct user research to understand needs and pain points
Design an information architecture and interaction flow informed by research
Build an interactive prototype in Figma
Conduct usability testing and iterate based on findings
deliverables: User research report with interview transcripts, affinity diagram, and persona
Information architecture: sitemap and user flows
Interactive Figma prototype with at least 10 linked screens
Usability test report with findings, severity ratings, and design iterations
Final presentation demonstrating the design evolution from research to refined prototype

criteria: At least 5 user interviews conducted with proper protocol
Affinity diagram synthesizes findings into clear themes
Persona is based on research patterns, not assumptions
Journey map identifies specific pain points and opportunities
Information architecture is logical and informed by card sorting
Wireframes communicate structure clearly
Interactive prototype supports all key task flows
Design follows established usability heuristics
Usability test conducted with at least 3 participants
Test tasks are realistic and measurable
Findings are prioritized by severity
Design is iterated based on test results with clear before/after
Design decisions are justified with research evidence
Presentation tells a clear story from research to design
Prototype is polished enough to communicate the concept
Documentation is complete and professionally formatted
Research-informed design demonstrating user-centered process
Well-structured information architecture and interaction design
Usability-tested prototype with evidence-based iterations
Professional documentation and presentation

midterm_examination.description: Written exam on HCI principles and methods

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering all HCI topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for usability testing sessions and design critiques.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Design projects are individual. Peer feedback during critiques is expected and graded.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All designs must be original work. Using UI kits and component libraries is allowed with attribution.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Figma (free education plan), a screen reader (VoiceOver or NVDA), and screen recording software.
